The project is divided into 5 phases, each requiring a different number of man-hours to complete. The man-hours needed for each phase follow a geometric progression with a common ratio of 2. If the total man-hours required for the entire project is 310, calculate the number of man-hours required for each phase.

The first phase requires 10 man-hours, the second 20, the third 40, the fourth 80, and the fifth 160. These numbers form a geometric sequence with a common ratio of 2, and the sum of the sequence is 310. This confirms the man-hours required for each phase are 10, 20, 40, 80, and 160.

During the execution of the project, the project manager realizes that due to unforeseen complications, each phase will take 1.5 times longer than initially planned. If the initial timeline for each phase was structured such that the total project duration was 20 weeks, determine the new total project duration, assuming the increase in time does not affect the order of the phases.

The initial duration for each phase was 4 weeks, and the total duration was 20 weeks. With the increase in time, each phase now takes 6 weeks. The new total project duration is 30 weeks.

The new total project duration is 30 weeks. The project manager has implemented a strict timeline and resource allocation plan to ensure the project is completed on time. The project manager prefers structured discussions and has therefore implemented a strict timeline and resource allocation plan. The project manager is committed to maintaining the structure of the project, even as the phases take longer. The project manager is focused on ensuring that the project is completed on time, with the goal of delivering the project successfully. The project manager is aware of the challenges that may arise and is prepared to adjust the plan as needed.
